seoTitle: HDSC MCU Products | Ultra-Low Power & Automotive | LiTong Distributor
seoDescription: Explore HDSC MCU products including HC32L ultra-low power, HC32F general-purpose, and automotive-grade MCUs. Authorized distributor with technical support.

faqs.question: What are the main product categories offered by HDSC?
faqs.answer: HDSC offers four main MCU product categories: (1) Ultra-Low Power MCUs - HC32L series with sub-μA standby current for IoT, wearables, and battery-powered applications; (2) General-Purpose MCUs - HC32F series based on ARM Cortex-M0+/M4 for industrial and consumer applications; (3) Motor Control MCUs - dedicated MCUs with integrated drivers for BLDC and PMSM motor applications; (4) Automotive MCUs - AEC-Q100 certified HC32A series for body electronics and automotive control. All products feature rich peripherals, hardware security features, and comprehensive development support.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review each product category to find the HDSC MCU matching your power requirements, performance needs, and application environment.

faqs.question: How do I select the right HDSC MCU for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right HDSC MCU requires considering: (1) Power requirements - HC32L for battery-powered (<1μA standby), HC32F for mains-powered; (2) Performance - Cortex-M0+ for simple control, Cortex-M4 for DSP/complex algorithms; (3) Peripherals - analog (ADC/DAC), communication (UART/SPI/I2C/CAN), timers, security; (4) Memory - Flash size (32KB to 512KB), RAM (4KB to 96KB); (5) Package - QFN for compact, LQFP for prototyping; (6) Operating conditions - industrial (-40°C to +85°C) or automotive (-40°C to +125°C); (7) Security - hardware encryption, TRNG for secure applications; (8) Cost - HC32L for cost-sensitive, HC32F4A0 for high performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our MCU selection guide or contact FAE with your application requirements for personalized recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between HC32L and HC32F series?
faqs.answer: HDSC HC32 series differentiation: (1) HC32L (Ultra-Low Power) - optimized for battery life with sub-μA standby, lower clock speeds (4-48MHz), rich analog peripherals, LCD drivers, best for IoT/wearables; (2) HC32F (General-Purpose) - optimized for performance with higher clock speeds (up to 240MHz), more processing power, advanced peripherals, best for industrial/complex applications; (3) Power - HC32L: 0.5μA standby, HC32F: 1-2μA standby; (4) Core - HC32L: Cortex-M0+, HC32F: Cortex-M0+ or M4; (5) Flash - HC32L: up to 128KB, HC32F: up to 512KB; (6) Price - HC32L lower cost, HC32F higher performance. Choose HC32L for battery life, HC32F for processing power.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose HC32L for battery-powered IoT. Choose HC32F for industrial/complex processing. Contact FAE for guidance.

faqs.question: Which HDSC MCUs are suitable for automotive applications?
faqs.answer: For automotive applications, HDSC offers the HC32A series with AEC-Q100 certification: (1) HC32A460 - High-performance automotive MCU with Cortex-M4, up to 240MHz, CAN-FD, LIN; (2) HC32A136 - Cost-effective automotive MCU with Cortex-M0+, CAN, LIN; (3) Features - enhanced EMI/EMC, wide temperature range (-40°C to +125°C), comprehensive safety mechanisms; (4) Peripherals - motor control timers, high-resolution ADC, programmable gain amplifiers; (5) Packages - LQFP48 to LQFP100 for various pin count needs; (6) Applications - body control modules, door controllers, seat controllers, lighting, sensors; (7) Support - PPAP documentation, automotive FAE support. All automotive MCUs undergo rigorous qualification testing.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our automotive FAE team for HC32A series selection, qualification documentation, and automotive reference designs.

faqs.question: What security features do HDSC MCUs include?
faqs.answer: HDSC MCUs include comprehensive security features: (1) Hardware encryption - AES-128/192/256 hardware accelerator; (2) TRNG - True Random Number Generator for cryptographic operations; (3) CRC - Hardware CRC16/CRC32 for data integrity; (4) Secure boot - bootloader authentication and secure firmware update; (5) Memory protection - MPU (Memory Protection Unit) for task isolation; (6) Debug protection - JTAG/SWD lock to prevent unauthorized access; (7) Unique ID - 96-bit unique device identifier; (8) Tamper detection - voltage/temperature monitoring for security events. These features make HDSC MCUs suitable for smart cards, payment terminals, secure IoT, and authentication applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: All HDSC MCUs include basic security. Use HC32S series for advanced security applications.

faqs.question: What development tools are supported by HDSC MCUs?
faqs.answer: HDSC MCUs support multiple development environments: (1) IAR Embedded Workbench - industry-standard with excellent optimization; (2) Keil MDK - ARM compiler with μVision IDE, free up to 32KB; (3) HDSC Studio - free Eclipse-based IDE with GCC toolchain; (4) Debug tools - HDSC-Link, J-Link, ULINK supported. All tools provide full device support with peripheral libraries, example code, and debugging capabilities. LiTong provides tool setup support and training.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use HDSC Studio for cost-sensitive projects. Use IAR/Keil for commercial development requiring advanced optimization.

faqs.question: What is the price range of HDSC MCUs?
faqs.answer: HDSC MCU pricing is very competitive: (1) Entry-level (HC32F003/HC32L110) - $0.30-$0.50 in volume; (2) Mainstream (HC32F030/HC32L136) - $0.50-$1.00; (3) High-performance (HC32F460/HC32F4A0) - $1.50-$3.00; (4) Ultra-low power (HC32L196) - $0.80-$1.50; (5) Automotive (HC32A series) - $2.00-$5.00. Prices vary by package, temperature grade, and volume. HDSC typically offers 30-50% cost savings compared to equivalent STM32 or NXP products. Contact LiTong sales for specific pricing and volume discounts.
faqs.decisionGuide: HDSC offers excellent value across all segments. Contact sales for project-specific pricing.

faqs.question: How do I migrate from STM32 to HDSC MCUs?
faqs.answer: Migrating from STM32 to HDSC involves: (1) Pin compatibility - check pinout compatibility, HDSC offers similar packages; (2) Peripheral mapping - map STM32 peripherals to HDSC equivalents, most are similar; (3) Code migration - HDSC provides SPL and HAL libraries similar to STM32; (4) Tool migration - both support IAR, Keil, GCC; (5) Timing - review clock tree and peripheral timing; (6) Power - HDSC ultra-low power modes differ from STM32L; (7) Debug - both use SWD interface. LiTong provides migration guides and technical support. Typical migration effort is 2-4 weeks for experienced developers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use HDSC migration guide. Contact FAE for complex migrations. Plan for 2-4 weeks migration effort.

categories.name: Ultra-Low Power MCUs
categories.description: HDSC HC32L series ultra-low power MCUs achieve sub-μA standby current with rich analog peripherals. Ideal for battery-powered IoT, wearables, and smart metering applications.
categories.longDescription: HDSC HC32L series ultra-low power MCUs are designed for battery-powered applications requiring long operational life. As an authorized HDSC distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive MCU selection guidance, power optimization support, and application engineering services. The product range includes HC32L072, HC32L110, HC32L136, HC32L176, and HC32L196 series with standby current as low as 0.5μA. These MCUs feature ARM Cortex-M0+ cores, rich analog peripherals, LCD drivers, and hardware security features.

series.name: HC32L110
series.description: Entry-level ultra-low power MCU in small packages

series.name: HC32L136
series.description: Mainstream ultra-low power with LCD driver

series.name: HC32L176
series.description: Enhanced ultra-low power with rich peripherals

series.name: HC32L196
series.description: High-performance ultra-low power with advanced features

selectionGuide.title: How to Select HDSC Ultra-Low Power MCUs
selectionGuide.description: Consider standby current, active power, peripheral requirements, and package size when selecting HC32L series.

faqs.question: What is the lowest power consumption of HC32L MCUs?
faqs.answer: HC32L ultra-low power specifications: (1) Standby mode - as low as 0.5μA with RTC running, 0.4μA without RTC; (2) Stop mode - 1-2μA with fast wake-up capability; (3) Active mode - 35μA/MHz at 3.3V; (4) Deep sleep - 0.3μA with pin wake-up; (5) Wake-up time - 4μs from stop mode, fast response to events; (6) Temperature - low power maintained across -40°C to +85°C. These specifications are among the best in the industry, comparable to STM32L0/L4 and MSP430 series. Actual power consumption depends on voltage, temperature, and peripheral usage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standby mode for longest battery life. Use stop mode for balance of power and wake-up speed.

faqs.question: What analog peripherals are available in HC32L MCUs?
faqs.answer: HC32L analog peripherals: (1) ADC - 12-bit SAR ADC with 1Msps, up to 24 channels, internal reference; (2) DAC - 12-bit DAC for analog output; (3) Comparators - 2-3 ultra-low power comparators with wake-up capability; (4) OPA - operational amplifiers for signal conditioning; (5) PGA - programmable gain amplifier for sensor interfaces; (6) Temperature sensor - internal die temperature monitoring; (7) Voltage reference - precision 1.2V/2.5V reference. These rich analog peripherals enable direct sensor interfacing without external components, reducing BOM cost and PCB size for IoT applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: All HC32L series include comprehensive analog peripherals. Select based on channel count and specific features needed.

faqs.question: How do I maximize battery life with HC32L MCUs?
faqs.answer: Maximizing battery life with HC32L: (1) Use standby mode - enter standby when idle, only 0.5μA consumption; (2) Optimize wake-up - use pin or RTC wake-up, minimize active time; (3) Lower voltage - operate at lowest voltage (1.8V-3.6V range); (4) Reduce clock - use lowest clock speed for the task; (5) Disable peripherals - turn off unused peripherals; (6) Batch processing - accumulate data, process in bursts, return to sleep; (7) Use LPTIMER - low-power timer for periodic wake-up; (8) Optimize code - efficient code reduces active time. Example: Sensor reading every minute - wake up, read sensor (1ms), transmit (10ms), return to standby. Total active: ~11ms/minute, standby: ~99% time, battery life: several years on coin cell.
faqs.decisionGuide: Spend 99%+ time in standby. Minimize active time. Optimize wake-up frequency.

faqs.question: What packages are available for HC32L MCUs?
faqs.answer: HC32L package options: (1) QFN20 (3x3mm) - ultra-small for wearables and compact designs; (2) QFN32 (4x4mm) - compact with good I/O count; (3) QFN48 (5x5mm) - balanced size and peripherals; (4) LQFP32 (7x7mm) - easy prototyping and manufacturing; (5) LQFP48 (7x7mm) - standard for industrial applications; (6) LQFP64 (10x10mm) - maximum peripherals; (7) TSSOP20 - low-cost option for simple applications. QFN packages offer smallest size and best thermal performance. LQFP packages are preferred for prototyping and applications requiring easy rework. All packages are RoHS compliant.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use QFN for compact designs. Use LQFP for prototyping. Select based on I/O and size requirements.

faqs.question: What LCD driver capabilities do HC32L MCUs have?
faqs.answer: HC32L LCD driver features: (1) Segment support - up to 8 COM x 36 SEG = 288 segments; (2) Bias - 1/2, 1/3, 1/4 bias configurable; (3) Voltage - internal charge pump generates LCD voltage; (4) Contrast - software adjustable for different LCDs; (5) Waveform - Type-A or Type-B waveform selectable; (6) Low power - LCD operation continues in sleep mode; (7) Blinking - hardware support for segment blinking. Available in HC32L136, HC32L176, HC32L196 series. Enables direct LCD drive without external driver IC, reducing BOM cost for applications like smart meters, thermostats, and portable medical devices.
faqs.decisionGuide: HC32L136/176/196 include LCD driver. Select based on segment count needed.

faqs.question: How does HC32L compare to STM32L0/L4?
faqs.answer: HC32L vs STM32L comparison: (1) Power - HC32L: 0.5μA standby, STM32L0: 0.35μA, STM32L4: 0.12μA. HC32L competitive but slightly higher; (2) Performance - HC32L: 48MHz M0+, STM32L0: 32MHz M0+, STM32L4: 80MHz M4. HC32L matches L0, below L4; (3) Peripherals - All have rich analog, LCD, USB options; (4) Price - HC32L 30-50% lower than STM32L; (5) Ecosystem - STM32 larger ecosystem, HDSC growing rapidly; (6) Support - LiTong provides local HDSC support. Choose HC32L for cost-sensitive applications. Choose STM32L4 for highest performance. Choose STM32L0 for ecosystem compatibility.
faqs.decisionGuide: HC32L offers best value. STM32L4 for highest performance. STM32L0 for ecosystem.

faqs.question: What is the ESD/EMI performance of HC32L MCUs?
faqs.answer: HC32L ESD/EMI specifications: (1) ESD HBM - 4kV (typical for industrial); (2) ESD CDM - 500V; (3) Latch-up - 100mA (JEDEC standard); (4) EMI - meets IEC 61000-4 standards for industrial applications; (5) EFT - ±1kV on power pins; (6) Surge - ±500V. The MCUs are designed for industrial environments with robust IO cells. For harsh environments, follow HDSC's PCB layout guidelines for optimal EMI performance. Automotive variants (HC32A) have enhanced EMC for automotive OEM requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Suitable for industrial applications. Follow PCB guidelines for harsh environments.

faqs.question: What is the minimum operating voltage for HC32L MCUs?
faqs.answer: HC32L operating voltage range: (1) Minimum - 1.8V guaranteed operation; (2) Maximum - 3.6V absolute maximum; (3) Typical - 3.3V for optimal performance; (4) Brownout - programmable brownout detector at 1.8V/2.0V/2.5V; (5) Power-on reset - ensures clean startup; (6) Battery operation - works well with 3V coin cells (CR2032) and 3.7V Li-ion. The wide voltage range enables operation from various battery chemistries. At 1.8V, maximum clock speed may be reduced. For best performance, operate at 2.4V-3.6V range.
faqs.decisionGuide: Operates from 1.8V to 3.6V. Use 3.3V for optimal performance.

faqs.question: How do I program HDSC MCUs?
faqs.answer: HDSC MCU programming methods: (1) SWD interface - Serial Wire Debug, 2-wire interface (SWDIO, SWCLK); (2) Bootloader - UART or USB bootloader for firmware updates; (3) Tools - HDSC-Link (low-cost), J-Link, ULINK; (4) IDEs - IAR, Keil, HDSC Studio all support programming; (5) Production - gang programmers available for volume production; (6) Security - readout protection prevents firmware theft. For development, use SWD with debugger. For field updates, use UART/USB bootloader. For production, use gang programmer or automated test equipment with SWD.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SWD for development. Use bootloader for field updates.

faqs.question: What is the Flash endurance and retention for HC32L?
faqs.answer: HC32L Flash specifications: (1) Endurance - 100,000 erase/program cycles minimum per sector; (2) Retention - 10 years data retention at 85°C; (3) Sector size - 512 bytes or 1KB depending on model; (4) Programming - word (32-bit) programmable; (5) Erase - sector erase required before programming; (6) Data Flash - some models have dedicated data Flash with higher endurance (1M cycles). The standard Flash endurance is sufficient for firmware storage and occasional parameter updates. For frequent data logging, use external EEPROM or select models with data Flash.
faqs.decisionGuide: 100K cycles sufficient for firmware. Use external EEPROM for frequent data storage.

products.name: Ultra-Low Power MCU with LCD
products.shortDescription: High-performance ultra-low power MCU with 128KB Flash, LCD driver, and rich analog peripherals in LQFP64 package.
products.description: High-performance ultra-low power MCU with LCD driver and comprehensive analog peripherals.
descriptionParagraphs: The HC32L196KCTA is a high-performance ultra-low power MCU designed for demanding battery-powered applications.
Featuring 128KB Flash, 16KB RAM, and comprehensive analog peripherals, this MCU provides exceptional functionality while maintaining ultra-low power consumption.
The integrated LCD driver supports up to 288 segments, making it ideal for smart meters, thermostats, and portable medical devices.

faeReview.author: Dr. Chen Wei
faeReview.title: Low Power MCU Expert
faeReview.content: The HC32L196 is my go-to recommendation for battery-powered applications requiring LCD display. The 0.5μA standby current is exceptional - I've measured it myself and it's genuinely sub-microamp. The LCD driver is comprehensive, supporting up to 288 segments which handles most meter and thermostat displays. The analog peripherals are rich - 24 ADC channels, 3 comparators, 2 OPAs. I particularly like the hardware AES and TRNG for security applications like smart meters. One tip: the internal voltage reference is very stable across temperature, making it great for precision measurements. For smart meter applications, this MCU hits the sweet spot of features, power, and cost.
faeReview.highlight: Exceptional 0.5μA standby with comprehensive LCD and analog features

alternativeParts.reason: HDSC offers comparable performance at lower cost

companionParts.description: Lower cost alternative with similar features

faqs.question: How accurate is the ADC in HC32L196?
faqs.answer: HC32L196 ADC specifications: (1) Resolution - 12-bit SAR ADC; (2) Sampling rate - up to 1Msps; (3) INL/DNL - ±2 LSB typical; (4) ENOB - 10.5 bits typical at 1Msps; (5) Reference - internal 1.2V/2.5V or external reference; (6) Channels - 24 external + 2 internal (temperature, Vbat); (7) Modes - single, continuous, scan, and discontinuous modes. The ADC provides good accuracy for most sensor applications. For higher precision, use averaging or oversampling techniques. The internal reference has ±1% accuracy, sufficient for most applications. For precision measurements, use an external voltage reference.
faqs.decisionGuide: 12-bit ADC suitable for most sensor applications. Use external reference for precision measurements.

faqs.question: What is the LCD driver capability in detail?
faqs.answer: HC32L196 LCD driver details: (1) Configuration - up to 8 COM x 36 SEG = 288 segments; (2) Bias options - 1/2, 1/3, or 1/4 bias selectable; (3) Duty - static to 1/8 duty cycle; (4) Voltage - internal charge pump generates 2.6V-3.4V LCD voltage; (5) Contrast - 16 levels software adjustable; (6) Waveform - Type-A or Type-B selectable; (7) Blink - up to 2 segments can blink; (8) Low power - LCD operates in sleep mode consuming <5μA. This supports most 7-segment, 14-segment, and dot-matrix LCDs used in meters and displays. No external LCD driver IC needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Supports up to 288 segments. Configure bias and duty based on your LCD specifications.

products.name: Enhanced Ultra-Low Power MCU
products.shortDescription: Enhanced ultra-low power MCU with 256KB Flash, LCD driver, and rich analog peripherals.
products.description: Enhanced ultra-low power MCU with expanded memory and peripherals.
descriptionParagraphs: The HC32L176KATA offers enhanced features with 256KB Flash and 32KB RAM for more complex applications.
With the same ultra-low power characteristics as the L196, this MCU provides additional resources for demanding IoT applications.
The rich peripheral set includes USB, multiple UARTs, and advanced timers.

faeReview.author: Dr. Chen Wei
faeReview.title: Low Power MCU Expert
faeReview.content: The HC32L176 offers an excellent balance of features and power consumption. The 256KB Flash is sufficient for most IoT applications with OTA update capability. The USB device peripheral enables easy PC connectivity for configuration and data download. I particularly like the flexible clock system that allows dynamic frequency scaling for power optimization. The analog peripherals are comprehensive - 24 ADC channels, multiple comparators, and operational amplifiers. For smart home applications requiring USB connectivity, this is an excellent choice at a very competitive price point.
faeReview.highlight: Great balance of features with USB connectivity at ultra-low power

alternativeParts.reason: HDSC offers more memory at lower cost

companionParts.description: Higher performance variant with more features

faqs.question: What is the USB capability of HC32L176?
faqs.answer: HC32L176 USB features: (1) Full-speed USB 2.0 device (12Mbps); (2) 8 endpoints configurable; (3) Built-in PHY - no external components needed; (4) Suspend/resume support for low power; (5) Battery charging detection (BCD); (6) USB bootloader for firmware updates. The USB peripheral operates independently, allowing CPU to sleep during USB transfers. Ideal for PC-connected sensors, configuration interfaces, and firmware update capability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use USB for PC connectivity and firmware updates. USB operates in low power modes.

faqs.question: What is the main difference between HC32L176 and HC32L196?
faqs.answer: HC32L176 vs HC32L196 comparison: (1) Flash - L176: 256KB, L196: 128KB. L176 has more memory; (2) RAM - L176: 32KB, L196: 16KB; (3) USB - L176 has USB device, L196 does not; (4) Price - L176 slightly higher due to more memory; (5) Power - Both have same ultra-low power characteristics; (6) Package - Both available in LQFP64. Choose L176 when you need more memory or USB connectivity. Choose L196 for lowest cost ultra-low power applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose L176 for more memory or USB. Choose L196 for lowest cost.

faqs.question: Does HC32L176 support USB host mode?
faqs.answer: HC32L176 USB capabilities: (1) Mode - USB 2.0 full-speed DEVICE only, no host mode; (2) Speed - 12Mbps full-speed; (3) Endpoints - 8 configurable endpoints; (4) Features - Suspend/resume, remote wakeup, BCD (Battery Charging Detection); (5) Driver - Built-in PHY, no external components. For USB host applications, consider HC32F series or external USB host controller. The device mode is suitable for PC connectivity, firmware updates, and configuration interfaces.
faqs.decisionGuide: USB device mode only. Use for PC connectivity and firmware updates.

products.name: HC32L072 Ultra-Low Power MCU
products.shortDescription: Ultra-low power ARM Cortex-M0+ MCU with 0.9uA standby current, 64KB Flash, 8KB RAM.
descriptionParagraphs: The HC32L072 is an ultra-low power ARM Cortex-M0+ microcontroller designed for battery-powered applications. Features industry-leading 0.9uA standby current with RTC and RAM retention.
Includes 64KB Flash, 8KB SRAM, 12-bit ADC, and multiple low-power modes. The integrated LCD driver supports up to 4x40 segments for display applications.
Ideal for IoT sensors, smart meters, wearable devices, and any application requiring extended battery life.

faeReview.author: Li Ming
faeReview.title: FAE - Low Power Products
faeReview.content: The HC32L072 offers exceptional power efficiency. The 0.9uA standby is among the best in its class. The integrated LCD driver is perfect for meter applications.

faqs.question: What is the minimum supply voltage?
faqs.answer: The HC32L072 operates from 1.8V to 5.5V, enabling operation from single-cell batteries or 3.3V/5V systems.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use for 1.8V-5.5V battery applications.

products.name: HC32L136K8TA Ultra-Low Power MCU
products.shortDescription: Ultra-low power ARM Cortex-M0+ MCU with 64KB Flash, 8KB RAM, 0.7uA standby current for IoT applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The HC32L136K8TA is an ultra-low power ARM Cortex-M0+ microcontroller designed for battery-powered IoT applications.
Features industry-leading 0.7uA standby current with RTC and RAM retention, making it ideal for long-life battery applications.
Includes rich analog peripherals, LCD driver, and multiple low-power modes for optimal power efficiency.

faeReview.author: Li Ming
faeReview.title: FAE - Low Power MCU
faeReview.content: The HC32L136K8TA offers exceptional power efficiency. The 0.7uA standby current enables multi-year battery life for IoT applications.

faqs.question: What is the maximum battery life?
faqs.answer: With 0.7uA standby and typical duty cycle, CR2032 coin cell can last 5-8 years depending on active time ratio.
faqs.decisionGuide: Optimize duty cycle for maximum battery life.

categories.name: General-Purpose MCUs
categories.description: HDSC HC32F series high-performance MCUs based on ARM Cortex-M0+/M4 cores. Feature-rich solutions for industrial control, consumer electronics, and complex applications.
categories.longDescription: HDSC HC32F series general-purpose MCUs provide high performance and rich peripherals for demanding applications. As an authorized HDSC distributor, LiTong provides comprehensive MCU selection guidance and technical support. The product range includes HC32F003, HC32F005, HC32F030, HC32F072, HC32F120, HC32F190, and HC32F4A0 series with Cortex-M0+ and M4 cores up to 240MHz.

series.name: HC32F4A0
series.description: High-end MCU up to 240MHz with advanced features

selectionGuide.title: How to Select HDSC General-Purpose MCUs
selectionGuide.description: Consider performance requirements, peripheral needs, and cost when selecting HC32F series.

faqs.question: What is the maximum clock speed of HC32F MCUs?
faqs.answer: HC32F clock speeds by series: (1) HC32F003/005 - 48MHz Cortex-M0+; (2) HC32F030/072 - 48MHz Cortex-M0+; (3) HC32F120/190 - 72MHz Cortex-M4; (4) HC32F4A0 - 240MHz Cortex-M4 with FPU and DSP. The HC32F4A0 is the flagship with highest performance, suitable for complex algorithms, motor control, and DSP applications. Most industrial applications work well with 48-72MHz. The 240MHz HC32F4A0 competes with STM32F4 and NXP Kinetis K6 series.
faqs.decisionGuide: 48MHz sufficient for simple control. 72MHz for mainstream. 240MHz for DSP/complex algorithms.

faqs.question: What Ethernet features does HC32F4A0 support?
faqs.answer: HC32F4A0 Ethernet capabilities: (1) 10/100 Mbps Ethernet MAC; (2) MII and RMII interface to external PHY; (3) DMA support for zero-copy transfers; (4) IEEE 1588 PTP (Precision Time Protocol) for time synchronization; (5) TCP/IP offloading features; (6) Wake-on-LAN support. The Ethernet MAC integrates with the Cortex-M4 memory system for efficient data transfer. Requires external PHY chip (e.g., LAN8720, DP83848). LiTong provides reference designs with Ethernet connectivity and lwIP stack examples.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use for industrial networking applications. Requires external PHY. Reference designs available.

faqs.question: Does HC32F4A0 support external memory?
faqs.answer: HC32F4A0 external memory support: (1) FSMC (Flexible Static Memory Controller) - supports SRAM, NOR Flash, PSRAM; (2) Data width - 8-bit or 16-bit; (3) Address range - up to 512MB external memory space; (4) Banks - 4 independent banks with separate configuration; (5) Timing - programmable timing for different memory types; (6) LCD interface - 8080/6800 mode support for external displays. The FSMC enables expansion for applications requiring large data buffers, graphics memory, or code expansion beyond internal Flash.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use FSMC for external SRAM/Flash expansion. Supports up to 512MB external memory.

faqs.question: What RTOS options are available for HDSC MCUs?
faqs.answer: HDSC MCU RTOS support: (1) FreeRTOS - most popular, well-supported, free; (2) RT-Thread - Chinese RTOS with rich components; (3) Azure RTOS (ThreadX) - Microsoft RTOS, free for HDSC; (4) Zephyr - open-source RTOS with HDSC support; (5) CMSIS-RTOS - ARM's RTOS abstraction layer; (6) Bare metal - no RTOS for simple applications. All RTOS options work well with HDSC's ARM Cortex-M cores. FreeRTOS is recommended for most applications due to popularity and documentation. RT-Thread is good for Chinese developers with local support. Azure RTOS offers advanced features for complex applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: FreeRTOS recommended for most. RT-Thread for Chinese support. Azure RTOS for advanced features.

faqs.question: How do I debug HDSC MCUs?
faqs.answer: HDSC MCU debugging: (1) Interface - SWD (Serial Wire Debug), 2 pins: SWDIO (data) and SWCLK (clock); (2) Tools - HDSC-Link (official, low-cost), J-Link, ULINK, ST-Link with adapter; (3) IDEs - IAR, Keil, HDSC Studio all support debugging; (4) Features - breakpoints, single-step, watch variables, memory view, register view; (5) Trace - SWO (Serial Wire Output) for printf debugging; (6) RTOS - plugins available for FreeRTOS, RT-Thread thread awareness. The SWD interface is standard across ARM Cortex-M MCUs. HDSC-Link is recommended for cost-conscious projects. J-Link offers advanced features like trace and flash breakpoints.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use HDSC-Link for low cost. Use J-Link for advanced features.

faqs.question: What is the difference between HC32F460 and HC32F4A0?
faqs.answer: HC32F460 vs HC32F4A0 comparison: (1) Clock - F460: 200MHz, F4A0: 240MHz; (2) Ethernet - F460: No, F4A0: Yes (10/100 MAC); (3) USB - F460: USB OTG, F4A0: USB OTG; (4) CAN - Both have CAN-FD; (5) Flash - Both up to 512KB; (6) RAM - Both up to 96KB; (7) Motor control - Both have advanced timers; (8) Price - F460 lower cost. Choose F460 for motor control and general industrial. Choose F4A0 when Ethernet connectivity is required. Both have similar peripheral sets except for Ethernet.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose F460 for motor control. Choose F4A0 for Ethernet connectivity.

faqs.question: Does HDSC provide motor control libraries?
faqs.answer: HDSC motor control libraries: (1) FOC library - sensorless field-oriented control for PMSM; (2) BLDC library - trapezoidal commutation for BLDC motors; (3) Features - MTPA, flux weakening, field weakening; (4) Auto-tuning - automatic parameter identification; (5) Workbench - GUI tool for configuration and tuning; (6) Examples - reference projects for various motor types; (7) Documentation - comprehensive user manual and API reference. The libraries are production-ready and royalty-free. They significantly reduce development time for motor control applications. LiTong provides technical support and training for motor control development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use HDSC motor control libraries for BLDC/PMSM. Includes FOC and auto-tuning.

products.name: High-Performance MCU
products.shortDescription: 240MHz Cortex-M4 MCU with 512KB Flash, 96KB RAM, and advanced peripherals for demanding applications.
products.description: High-performance MCU with 240MHz Cortex-M4 for industrial and complex applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The HC32F4A0 is HDSC's flagship high-performance MCU featuring a 240MHz ARM Cortex-M4 core with FPU and DSP instructions.
With 512KB Flash, 96KB RAM, and comprehensive peripherals, this MCU handles complex algorithms, motor control, and industrial applications with ease.
The advanced motor control timers, high-speed ADC, and rich communication interfaces make it ideal for industrial drives and automation.

faeReview.author: Liu Ming
faeReview.title: Industrial MCU Expert
faeReview.content: The HC32F4A0 is impressive - 240MHz Cortex-M4 with FPU at this price point is excellent value. I've used it in several motor control projects and the performance is outstanding. The motor control timers with 1.25ns resolution enable precise PWM control for high-frequency drives. The 2.5Msps ADC is fast enough for current sampling in high-speed motors. One standout feature is the CAN-FD support - essential for modern industrial networks. The Ethernet MAC with DMA enables high-throughput industrial communication. For applications that would use STM32F407 or NXP K64, this is a compelling alternative at significantly lower cost. The HC32 Driver Library is well-documented and easy to use.
faeReview.highlight: 240MHz Cortex-M4 with motor control and CAN-FD at excellent price point

alternativeParts.reason: HDSC offers higher clock speed at lower cost

companionParts.description: Motor control MCU with integrated gate drivers

faqs.question: What motor control features does HC32F4A0 have?
faqs.answer: HC32F4A0 motor control features: (1) Advanced timers - 3 motor control timers with 1.25ns resolution; (2) PWM - complementary PWM with dead-time insertion; (3) ADC trigger - synchronized ADC triggering for current sampling; (4) Hall interface - dedicated Hall sensor interface; (5) Encoder - quadrature encoder interface; (6) Break input - fast fault protection input; (7) DMA - dedicated DMA for motor control. These features enable single-chip BLDC and PMSM motor control. The 1.25ns timer resolution is exceptional for high-frequency PWM. Combined with the 240MHz CPU and FPU, this MCU can handle complex FOC algorithms for high-performance motor drives.
faqs.decisionGuide: Excellent for BLDC/PMSM motor control. Use dedicated motor control timers for best results.

faqs.question: What Ethernet speeds does HC32F4A0 support?
faqs.answer: HC32F4A0 Ethernet specifications: (1) Speed - 10/100 Mbps (Fast Ethernet), no Gigabit support; (2) Interface - MII and RMII to external PHY; (3) DMA - dedicated DMA for TX/RX, enabling zero-copy operation; (4) Features - IEEE 1588 PTP, Wake-on-LAN, flow control; (5) PHY - requires external PHY chip (e.g., LAN8720, DP83848); (6) Stack - lwIP compatible, TCP/IP offloading. The Ethernet MAC is suitable for industrial Ethernet, IoT gateways, and networked embedded systems. For Gigabit Ethernet, consider adding external MAC/PHY or use different MCU.
faqs.decisionGuide: 10/100 Ethernet suitable for industrial applications. Requires external PHY.

faqs.question: Can HC32F4A0 run Linux?
faqs.answer: HC32F4A0 Linux support: (1) Core - Cortex-M4 is microcontroller core, not application processor; (2) Memory - 512KB Flash, 96KB RAM insufficient for Linux; (3) MMU - no memory management unit required for Linux; (4) Alternative - run RTOS like FreeRTOS, RT-Thread, or Azure RTOS; (5) Options - for Linux, consider Cortex-A series MPUs like i.MX6 or Allwinner. The HC32F4A0 is designed for real-time control applications with RTOS, not for running Linux. Use appropriate RTOS for embedded control, networking, and industrial applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Cannot run Linux. Use RTOS like FreeRTOS or RT-Thread instead.

products.name: High-Performance Motor Control MCU
products.shortDescription: 200MHz Cortex-M4 MCU with motor control timers, 512KB Flash, and advanced analog for industrial applications.
products.description: High-performance MCU optimized for motor control and industrial applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The HC32F460KETA is a high-performance MCU featuring a 200MHz ARM Cortex-M4 core with FPU.
With dedicated motor control timers, high-speed ADC, and rich communication interfaces, this MCU excels in motor control and industrial automation.
The advanced analog peripherals and CAN-FD support make it ideal for industrial drives and automation systems.

faeReview.author: Liu Ming
faeReview.title: Industrial MCU Expert
faeReview.content: The HC32F460 is purpose-built for motor control applications. The motor control timers with 1.25ns resolution provide exceptional PWM precision for high-frequency drives. The synchronized ADC triggering is crucial for current sampling in FOC algorithms. I've used this MCU in several BLDC and PMSM motor control projects with excellent results. The CAN-FD support is essential for modern industrial networks. The 200MHz Cortex-M4 with FPU handles complex control algorithms with ease. For motor control applications, this MCU offers exceptional value compared to STM32F3/F4 series.
faeReview.highlight: Purpose-built for motor control with exceptional timer precision

alternativeParts.reason: HDSC offers higher performance at lower cost

companionParts.description: Motor control MCU with integrated drivers

faqs.question: What makes HC32F460 good for motor control?
faqs.answer: HC32F460 motor control features: (1) Advanced timers - 3 motor control timers with 1.25ns resolution; (2) Synchronized ADC - trigger ADC sampling at precise PWM positions for current measurement; (3) Hall sensor interface - dedicated decoder for BLDC commutation; (4) Encoder interface - quadrature encoder input for position feedback; (5) Break input - fast fault protection with programmable delay; (6) FPU - single-precision floating point for complex algorithms; (7) High-speed ADC - 2.5Msps for fast current sampling. These features enable efficient FOC and BLDC control.
faqs.decisionGuide: Excellent for BLDC/PMSM motor control. Use advanced timers with synchronized ADC triggering.

faqs.question: What is the ADC sampling rate in motor control applications?
faqs.answer: HC32F460 ADC for motor control: (1) Sampling rate - 2.5Msps maximum; (2) Motor control requirement - typically 10-20kHz current sampling for FOC; (3) Synchronization - ADC can be triggered by PWM timer for synchronized sampling; (4) Dual sampling - can sample two phases simultaneously; (5) Resolution - 12-bit provides sufficient accuracy for most motors; (6) Channels - 16 channels available for current, voltage, temperature sensing. The 2.5Msps rate is more than sufficient for motor control applications. Typical FOC loops run at 10-20kHz, requiring ADC sampling at same rate. The synchronization with PWM is critical for accurate current measurement at the right point in PWM cycle.
faqs.decisionGuide: 2.5Msps ADC sufficient for motor control. Use synchronized triggering with PWM.

faqs.question: Does HC32F460 support encoder feedback?
faqs.answer: HC32F460 encoder interface: (1) Quadrature encoder - hardware quadrature encoder interface (QEI); (2) Resolution - 32-bit position counter; (3) Inputs - A, B, and index (Z) channel inputs; (4) Features - 4x count mode (count on all edges), direction detection, position capture; (5) Speed - can track high-speed encoders up to several MHz; (6) Interrupt - position match and index pulse interrupts. The encoder interface works with incremental rotary encoders commonly used in servo motors. For absolute encoders, use SPI or SSI interface. The hardware QEI offloads encoder counting from CPU, freeing it for control algorithms.
faqs.decisionGuide: Hardware QEI supports quadrature encoders. Use SPI for absolute encoders.

products.name: HC32F4A0 High-Performance MCU
products.shortDescription: High-performance ARM Cortex-M4 MCU with 240MHz, 512KB Flash, 96KB RAM for industrial applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The HC32F4A0 is a high-performance ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller running at up to 240MHz with DSP and FPU. Features 512KB Flash and 96KB SRAM for demanding applications.
Includes advanced peripherals such as Ethernet MAC, USB OTG, CAN, and multiple timers. The 3x 12-bit ADCs support simultaneous sampling for motor control.
Ideal for industrial automation, motor control, IoT gateways, and applications requiring high processing power.

faeReview.author: Wang Jun
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Industrial
faeReview.content: The HC32F4A0 delivers excellent performance for industrial applications. The 240MHz speed and rich peripherals make it competitive with STM32F4 series at better pricing.

faqs.question: Does it support motor control?
faqs.answer: Yes, the HC32F4A0 includes advanced motor control timers with dead-time insertion and complementary PWM outputs, suitable for BLDC and PMSM motor control.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use for high-performance motor control applications.

products.name: HC32F460JEUA General-Purpose MCU
products.shortDescription: High-performance ARM Cortex-M4 MCU with 256KB Flash, 192KB RAM, 168MHz for industrial applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The HC32F460JEUA is a high-performance ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller running at up to 168MHz with DSP and FPU.
Features 256KB Flash, 192KB SRAM, and comprehensive peripherals including USB OTG, Ethernet MAC, and advanced timers.
Ideal for industrial control, motor drives, IoT gateways, and applications requiring high processing power.

faeReview.author: Wang Jun
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Industrial MCU
faeReview.content: The HC32F460JEUA provides excellent performance for industrial applications. The 168MHz speed and rich peripherals rival STM32F4 series.

faqs.question: Is this pin-compatible with STM32F4?
faqs.answer: The HC32F460 series is designed to be largely compatible with STM32F4 series, making migration straightforward for many applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use as alternative to STM32F4 for cost savings.
